section.title: How Lovely Is Your Dwelling Place
section.sectionRationale: A Korahite pilgrim song of longing for God's own presence, moving from the sparrow's nest near the altar to the pilgrim road through the Valley of Baca, and climaxing in the conviction that one day in God's courts outweighs a thousand anywhere else.
section.thesis: The soul's deepest longing is satisfied not by circumstance or status but by nearness to the living God Himself, whose presence turns even the driest valley into springs and makes a single day at His threshold better than a lifetime elsewhere.
section.frameNote: Five movements: longing for God's dwelling, 1-4; the strengthened pilgrim on the road to Zion, 5-7; a prayer for God's anointed, 8-9; the surpassing worth of God's presence, 10-11; and the closing blessing on all who trust Him, 12.

units.label: Longing for God's dwelling
units.note: even the sparrow finds a home near the altar

verses.text: How lovely is Your dwelling place, O LORD of Hosts!
My soul {{kasaph|longs}}, even faints, for the courts of the LORD; my heart and my flesh cry out for the living God.
Even the sparrow has found a home, and the swallow a nest for herself, where she places her young near Your altars, O LORD of Hosts, my King and my God.
How blessed are those who dwell in Your house! They are ever praising You. Selah

units.label: The strengthened pilgrim
units.note: from the Valley of Baca to Zion

verses.text: Blessed are those whose strength is in You, whose hearts are set on pilgrimage.
As they pass through the {{baca|Valley of Baca}}, they make it a place of springs; even the autumn rain covers it with pools.
They go from strength to strength, until each appears before God in Zion.

units.label: A prayer for God's anointed
units.note: hear my prayer, look with favor

verses.text: O LORD God of Hosts, hear my prayer; give ear, O God of Jacob. Selah
Take notice of our shield, O God, and look with favor on the face of Your {{mashiach|anointed}}.

units.label: Better is one day
units.note: the surpassing worth of God's presence

verses.text: For better is one day in Your courts than a thousand elsewhere. I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God than dwell in the tents of the wicked.
For the LORD God is a sun and a shield; the LORD gives grace and glory; He withholds no good thing from those who walk with integrity.

units.label: Blessed is the man who trusts in You

verses.text: O LORD of Hosts, how blessed is the man who trusts in You!

kasaph.gloss: To long for, yearn, even faint with desire. The psalmist's soul does not merely wish for God's courts but is physically overwhelmed with longing for them, heart and flesh both crying out for the living God.

baca.gloss: Baca, related to the Hebrew word for weeping, names a dry, difficult valley on the pilgrim road. Those journeying to God in strength transform even this place of weeping into a place of springs.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one, Messiah. The prayer to 'look with favor on the face of Your anointed' names the reigning Davidic king in its original setting, while pointing forward to God's ultimate Anointed One, Jesus Christ.

basic.context: Sung by the sons of Korah, this psalm bursts with longing for God's own dwelling place, moving from the picture of a sparrow nesting safely near the altar, to pilgrims whose strength turns a dry valley into springs on their way to Zion, to the ringing conclusion that a single day near God outweighs a thousand anywhere else.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a pilgrim's song meant to be sung on the road and at journey's end, teaching that longing for God's presence itself, not merely the benefits that come with it, is the heart of true devotion.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalmist's soul does not merely want but kasaph, longs and even faints, for God's courts. Pilgrims pass through baca, a dry valley whose very name recalls weeping, yet turn it into springs by the strength God gives. The prayer for God's mashiach, anointed one, names Israel's king while reaching toward the final Anointed One, Christ.
basic.typology: The sparrow finding a home near God's altar anticipates the access every believer now has to God through Christ. The pilgrim road through the Valley of Baca, transformed into springs, previews the pattern of hardship turned to blessing fulfilled supremely in the sufferings and glory of Christ.
basic.christ: The prayer to look with favor on God's anointed, verse 9, reaches its fullest answer in Jesus, God's ultimate Anointed One, and the psalm's ache to dwell in God's house is answered in Christ's promise of many rooms in the Father's house, John 14:2, and God's dwelling finally among His people, Revelation 21:3.
basic.god: God is the living God whose very presence, not merely His gifts, satisfies the soul; He is sun and shield, giving grace and glory and withholding no good thing from those who walk with integrity, strengthening pilgrims for every step of the journey toward Him.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 27:4 shares this same single desire, to dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of one's life. John 14:2 answers this psalm's longing with Jesus' promise of a place in the Father's house. Revelation 21:3 pictures the final and permanent fulfillment, God's dwelling place now among men forever.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's longing to dwell near God, and its confidence that one day in His presence outweighs a thousand elsewhere, is answered fully in Christ, who opens the way into God's presence forever and in whom the deepest ache of every pilgrim heart finds its rest.

context.literary: A pilgrim song in five movements: longing for God's dwelling, 1-4; the strengthened pilgrim's road, 5-7; prayer for God's anointed, 8-9; the surpassing worth of God's presence, 10-11; and a closing blessing on all who trust Him, 12.
context.historical: Composed by the sons of Korah, Levitical gatekeepers and singers whose daily service explains their vivid longing for the sanctuary, likely sung by pilgrims journeying up to Jerusalem for the three annual feasts, Deuteronomy 16:16.
context.canonical: A close companion to Psalm 27 and Psalm 42 in its longing for God's presence, and part of the Korahite collection that consistently prizes nearness to God above every other good.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice, and to invite God's people to share, an all-consuming longing for God's own presence, and to teach that the pilgrim road, however dry or difficult, is transformed by the strength God supplies to those journeying toward Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes the specific experience and practice of pilgrimage to the Jerusalem temple; it prescribes as an abiding pattern that longing for God's presence itself, not merely His blessings, should be the chief desire of His people in every generation.

debates.question: Is the Valley of Baca, verse 6, a specific, identifiable geographic location or primarily a symbolic name for hardship on the pilgrim road?

views.label: A real, specific valley along one of the pilgrimage routes to Jerusalem, whose dry conditions made the transformation into pools of water a memorable, literal picture
views.case: The psalm elsewhere names concrete details of temple worship and pilgrimage, suggesting a real geographic setting rather than pure allegory.

views.label: A symbolic name, since 'Baca' echoes the Hebrew word for weeping, representing any place of hardship the pilgrim must pass through on the way to God
views.case: No valley by this exact name is confidently identified archaeologically, and the wordplay with weeping fits the psalm's larger theme of strength transforming hardship into blessing.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the same pastoral truth, that God supplies strength to transform real hardship into a place of provision for those journeying toward His presence.

study.originalLanguages: The psalmist's soul does more than wish, it kasaph, longs and faints, for God's courts, heart and flesh together. Pilgrims cross baca, a valley whose name echoes weeping, yet turn it into springs by strength God supplies. The prayer for God's mashiach, anointed one, names the reigning king while opening toward the final Messiah, Christ.

typology.type: The sparrow finding a home near God's altar, verse 3
typology.fulfillment: The access every believer now has into God's presence through Christ, who opens the way to the Father.

typology.type: The Valley of Baca transformed into springs, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of hardship turned to blessing fulfilled supremely in the suffering and glory of Christ, and in the pilgrim path of every believer who follows Him.

study.christ: The psalm's prayer, 'look with favor on the face of Your anointed,' verse 9, names Israel's king in its original setting and reaches its fullest answer in Jesus, God's ultimate Anointed One. The psalmist's longing to dwell in God's house is answered in Christ's promise of many rooms in His Father's house, John 14:2, and finds its final fulfillment when God's dwelling place is forever among His people, Revelation 21:3.
study.god: God is the living God whose presence alone satisfies the soul more than any circumstance, gift, or status; He is sun and shield to His people, giving grace and glory freely and withholding no good thing from those who walk with integrity, and He strengthens every pilgrim for the road that leads to Him.

crossReferences.note: Shares this psalm's single desire, to dwell in the house of the LORD and gaze on His beauty all the days of one's life.
Jesus answers this psalm's longing with His promise of a place in the Father's house.
Pictures the final, permanent fulfillment of this psalm's ache, God's dwelling place forever among His people.

study.oneStory: Psalm 84's longing to dwell near God, and its conviction that a single day in His presence outweighs a thousand elsewhere, is answered fully in Christ, who opens the way into the Father's house forever and satisfies the deepest ache of every pilgrim heart.

quiz.q: How does the psalmist describe his desire for God's dwelling place, verse 2?

options.text: His soul longs and even faints for the courts of the LORD; his heart and flesh cry out for the living God.
options.note: Verse 2 describes this all-consuming, physical longing directly.

options.text: He feels entirely indifferent to the temple.
options.note: The opposite is true; the psalmist expresses overwhelming longing.

options.text: He longs for it only when convenient.
options.note: The longing described is constant and overwhelming, not merely occasional.

options.text: He prefers his own house to God's courts.
options.note: The psalm expresses the opposite preference, for God's courts above all else.

quiz.q: What happens to the Valley of Baca as pilgrims pass through it, verse 6?

options.text: They make it a place of springs, and the autumn rain covers it with pools.
options.note: Verse 6 states this transformation directly.

options.text: It remains permanently dry and unusable.
options.note: The verse describes a transformation into springs, not continued dryness.

options.text: The pilgrims avoid it entirely by another route.
options.note: The pilgrims pass directly through it, transforming it rather than avoiding it.

options.text: It becomes more dangerous the longer they travel.
options.note: The valley becomes a source of provision, not increasing danger.

quiz.q: What request does the psalmist make regarding God's anointed, verse 9?

options.text: That God would look with favor on the face of His anointed.
options.note: Verse 9 makes this specific request.

options.text: That God would remove the anointed king from office.
options.note: The request is for God's favor on the anointed, not his removal.

options.text: That the anointed one would rule forever without any need of God's favor.
options.note: The prayer explicitly asks for God's favor, implying dependence, not self-sufficiency.

options.text: That a new anointed one be chosen immediately.
options.note: The request is favor on the existing anointed one, not his replacement.

quiz.q: How does the psalmist compare a day in God's courts to life elsewhere, verse 10?

options.text: Better is one day in God's courts than a thousand elsewhere; he would rather be a doorkeeper there than dwell in the tents of the wicked.
options.note: Verse 10 states this comparison exactly.

options.text: A thousand days anywhere else are better than one day with God.
options.note: This reverses the psalm's actual claim, which favors God's presence above all else.

options.text: There is no real difference between God's courts and any other place.
options.note: The psalm draws a sharp, deliberate contrast, not equivalence.

options.text: He would rather rule in the tents of the wicked than serve in God's house.
options.note: The psalm states the opposite preference explicitly.

quiz.q: What does the psalm say God is to those who walk with integrity, verse 11?

options.text: A sun and a shield, giving grace and glory and withholding no good thing.
options.note: Verse 11 describes God in exactly these terms.

options.text: A distant, uninvolved observer.
options.note: The verse describes intimate provision and protection, not distance.

options.text: A source of good things only for the wealthy.
options.note: The promise is for those who walk with integrity, not based on wealth.

options.text: A judge who withholds every good thing until judgment day.
options.note: The verse says God withholds no good thing now from the upright, not that He delays every blessing.

questions: The psalmist's soul 'longs, even faints' for God's presence, verse 2. How would you describe your own longing for God right now, and what would it look like to cultivate more of it?
Even a sparrow finds safety and a home near God's altar, verse 3. Where do you need to trust that you, too, have a secure home in God's presence, whatever your circumstances?

Pilgrims turn the dry Valley of Baca into springs by the strength God supplies, verse 6. What 'valley of weeping' are you walking through right now, and how might God's strength transform it into a place of provision?
The psalmist declares one day in God's courts better than a thousand elsewhere, verse 10. What competing 'thousand days' elsewhere are you tempted to value more highly than nearness to God?
The psalm ends by blessing 'the man who trusts in You,' verse 12. What specific area of your life right now calls for simple, renewed trust in God rather than anxious self-reliance?

translationNotes.note: 'Valley of Baca' can also be rendered 'Valley of Poplars'; either way, the name's likely echo of the Hebrew word for weeping fits the psalm's picture of hardship transformed into blessing.
'Covers it with pools' can also be rendered 'covers it with blessings,' both readings pointing to abundant provision along the pilgrim road.

'Your anointed' (mashiach) names the reigning Davidic king in its original setting, historically likely sung on his behalf during a pilgrimage festival, while opening forward toward God's ultimate Anointed One, Jesus Christ.

No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) could be confirmed specifically on Psalm 84 this session, so sermon status is 'none'.
